data.title: CoreWeave: A $100B+ Problem (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Circular financing is back in the spotlight after Nvidia Corporation’s (NVDA) $2B equity investment in CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), building on its earlier $6.3 billion initial capacity order.

I'll be blunt: I strongly believe the AI bears are wrong (at best) with the circular deals argument. In fact, I argue that anyone who is using this argument on CoreWeave as a pillar for a bear case hasn't really run the numbers.

This article addresses the circular financing fallacy and why the Nvidia contribution is mostly noise compared to what I estimate as a $100B+ in cumulative funding deficit through 2030.

Aside from this hot topic, I will cover some of the key items to monitor in the Q4 earnings release. I advance in this intro that my downgrade has to do with the uncertainty regarding the delay in the buildout due to a third-party builder. I need to see proof that this bottleneck is fully cleared before upgrading to a Strong Buy.

The Circular Financing Nonsense Is Back In The Spotlight

Headlines of circular financing in the AI space are back after Nvidia's $2B equity investment in CoreWeave.

Bear in mind that this is not the first deal between the two companies. Back in September, CoreWeave signed a $6.3B capacity agreement with Nvidia, where Nvidia is obligated to buy unused capacity through April 13, 2032.

In a Bloomberg interview, CEO Jensen Huang addressed the circular financing concerns with the following statement:

But it's a small percentage of the amount of money that they ultimately have to go raise, and so the idea that it is circular is — it's ridiculous.

My first question was, how small is the $2B plus the earlier $6.3B compared to the money CoreWeave needs to raise?

Let's estimate that.

CoreWeave's long-term goal is to reach more than 5 gigawatts in AI data center capacity by 2030. At the end of Q3 2025, the company disclosed 590 MW in active power across 41 data centers on both sides of the Atlantic and 2.9 GW in contracted power.

From a deployment timeline perspective, this remark from the CEO is key:

And as Nitin said, we expect the overwhelming majority of that 2.9 gigawatts of power to be brought into service over the next 12 to 24 months.

So, by the start of 2028, the company should have 3.5GW of active power, with the remaining 1.5 GW to be deployed in the next two years. Let's look at the costs now.

In the latest outlook, FY 2025 CapEx was guided between $12-$14B, with FY 2026 CapEx expected to be well in excess of double that of 2025. In my view, assuming a range of $24-$35B in FY26 CapEx is reasonable.

Now, in a conservative scenario (I'm being generous with the AI bears here), this CapEx range should stay consistent until the end of the decade.

It won't. I strongly think it will accelerate. However, that acceleration is impossible to model, as it depends on multiple factors, including hyperscaler spending, which is mainly tied to AI adoption among consumers and enterprises. All in all, I think the 2026-2030 CapEx could be anywhere between $120-$175B.

On top of that, don't forget about principal payments on the existing debt (a big theme among the bears). At the end of last quarter, total debt principal was $14.205B, with the following scheduled principal payments by year:

Assuming approximately $10B in principal payments until 2030 (bear in mind that they may refinance and push the maturities forward), the company's capital requirements jump to a range of $130-$185B.

The question now is how much operating cash flow CoreWeave is going to generate over the same period. Again, modeling this figure with accuracy is nearly impossible. However, assuming a 20%-30% operating cash flow margin (I am being generous with the AI bears here, given the 42% OCF margin for the 9-month 2025 period) on $30B in revenue per year, gives me $6-$9B in annual operating cash flows. Over 5 years, that's $30-$45B in OCF.

Side note:

Why $30B in revenue? Take a look at the Street's projections over the next 5 years:

Overall, I obtain $150B in revenue between 2026 and 2030. Annualized, that's $30B.

Back to my point. If I subtract the OCF from my estimated capital required, I obtain a deficit of $100B at the lower end and $140B at the higher end.

Now, compare that with Nvidia's $2B investment. It's peanuts!

Even if you add up the earlier $6.3B, the amount is negligible compared to the $100B deficit.

Therefore, I strongly believe that people who cite the AI circular deal as a pillar of the bear case on CoreWeave aren’t aware of how insignificant Nvidia is in the overall picture.

To be direct, my concern is how CoreWeave will find at least $100B by the end of the decade to finance its buildout. That’s a valid pillar for the bear case! To me, circularity is just a buzzword in the headlines meant to capture the general public’s interest.

Overall, I think Nvidia's investments ($2B and the ealrier $6.3B backstop) made complete sense. Why?

A quick look at the Q3 2025 10-Q filing reveals the following statement (emphasis added):

as a result of our obligations in our current customer contracts, all of the GPUs used in our infrastructure today are NVIDIA GPUs

Now, guess who the company’s largest customer is? Bingo! Microsoft (MSFT). In FY24, Microsoft accounted for 62% of the revenue. In Q3, "customer A" (unnamed, but I assume it is still Microsoft) represented 67% of total revenue.

Now, guess what the driver behind Microsoft's AI chip strategy is? Exactly. To rely less on Nvidia and the neoclouds. Interestingly enough, Microsoft released on the same day as the Nvidia $2B announcement an update regarding Maia 200. I argue the timing wasn't a mere coincidence. According to the release, Microsoft will initially deploy this chip at its Iowa data center, with expansion planned to Arizona. The risk here for CoreWeave is that Microsoft is increasingly relying less on Nvidia's GPUs. Again, this would be another valid pillar for the bear case on CoreWeave. Not the AI circular deals.

Q4 2025 Preview

With Q4 results around the corner, here is what I think are the most important items to monitor.

First, I would closely monitor the updates on the third-party developer who was behind schedule last year. As you may remember, this impacted Q4 expectations, and the company had to lower the FY25 guidance to $5.05–$5.15B (from $5.15–$5.35B).

Q4 is a key quarter for the top line of the company. So far, the company reported only $3.56B in revenue in the first nine months of 2025. Therefore, to meet the FY guide, Q4 revenue needs to be $1.49–$1.59B.

The CapEx guidance for 2026 is another key metric to monitor. Here, you want to see a meaningful jump compared to the $12–$14B FY 2025 range. As I said in the last section, management expects FY26 CapEx to be well in excess of double that of 2025. My estimate is $24-$35B.

Given the 271% YOY increase in the last quarter, revenue backlog is another metric to track in the upcoming release. As a side note, the company defines this metric as RPO + other estimated future revenue under committed contracts. The mix in Q3 was $50B in RPO and $5.6B in other estimated future revenue.

Finally, I would monitor any updates on active and contracted power. As I said earlier, the company expects to deploy the 2.9GW over the next 12-24 months. I would look for any updates on this timeframe, especially if expectations move on the back half of that range due to the recent third-party delays.

Valuation

As I'm typing, Seeking Alpha is showing a forward EV/Sales multiple of 13x. In comparison, Nebius has a forward EV/Sales multiple of 41x, more than 3x CoreWeave's.

Given the explosive growth projected in the next 12 months (with 5 consecutive triple-digit growth quarters), I think CoreWeave's valuation makes sense.

Conclusion

I still feel optimistic on CoreWeave. That said, I downgrade my rating to a Buy heading into the Q4 print (date still unannounced).

There is simply no way to know if that third-party builder has fully caught up yet, which is why I’m not comfortable staying more aggressive heading into the print. If the delays persisted into Q4, it could show up in slower deployments and deferred revenue recognition in 2026.

My downgrade has absolutely nothing to do with the recent Nvidia $2B equity investment. In my view, the Nvidia circular financing narrative is misleading. Even if you combine the $2B equity investment with the earlier $6.3B backstop, it’s immaterial versus the company’s $100B+ cumulative funding deficit through 2030 (according to my estimates). The real debate is how CoreWeave will fill the $100B gap, not whether Nvidia chips in with pocket change.

data.title: Essential Utilities: A Quality Dividend Grower To Buy Now
data.description:
In the world of income investing, few things are as comforting as a Dividend Champion (a publicly traded company with at least 25 straight years of dividend growth) trading at a discount. This is especially the case when one could soon become the undisputed water utility juggernaut.

Last October, the water utility sector was reshaped when Essential Utilities, Inc. (WTRG) announced a definitive agreement to merge with American Water Works (AWK). The $63 billion all-stock acquisition is designed to create a best-in-class utility titan, blending AWK's considerable scale with WTRG's operational expertise. The latter would receive 0.305 shares of AWK for each share of.

When I last covered WTRG for Brad Thomas with a Buy rating in August, I appreciated its status as a Dividend Champion. I liked that WTRG maintained its annual adjusted diluted EPS growth target of 5% to 7%. Its A- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook was another plus. Lastly, shares were priced moderately below my fair value estimate.

Today, I'm reaffirming my Buy rating. Once the deal closes, WTRG's annual EPS growth outlook would improve further to 7% to 9%. It will also benefit from an improvement in its cost of capital from an upgrade in its S&P credit rating to A. Shares of both WTRG and AWK are trading at double-digit percentage discounts to my fair value estimates, making the former attractive, no matter what happens with the deal.

A Big Upgrade To The Growth Outlook

On November 4th, WTRG shared its financial results for the third quarter ended September 30th, 2025. The company's total operating revenue surged 9.6% over the year-ago period to $477 million in the quarter. Put into perspective, that topped Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter by $4.6 million.

What was behind WTRG's respectable operating revenue growth rate for the third quarter? In absolute dollar growth, the company's Regulated Water segment led the way in the quarter, growing operating revenue by 8.8% to $364.1 million. This was mostly powered by higher water and wastewater rates, as well as a larger customer base stemming from acquisitions and organic growth, and an increase in volume consumption.

WTRG's Regulated Natural Gas segment was also a strong contributor during the third quarter. The segment's total operating revenue jumped 12.1% over the year-ago period to $108.5 million for the quarter. That was mostly fueled by a mix of higher rates, a lower tax repair surcredit, and an increase in purchased gas costs (which are passed onto customers due to WTRG's regulated utility business model).

WTRG's diluted EPS climbed 32% year-over-year to $0.33 in the third quarter. This exceeded Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter by $0.05. Higher rates, customer growth, and greater water volume more than offset higher expenses and reduced gas volume for the quarter. This is how diluted EPS growth exceeded total operating revenue growth in the quarter.

The benefits of the pending merger for WTRG would be similar to what I explained in a recent Buy article on AWK for Scott Kaufman (Treading Softly).

The combined company would serve 4.7 million water/wastewater connections across 17 states and 18 military installations. Its rate base as of December 31st, 2024, would be almost $30 billion. Naturally, that would drastically reduce the reliance of WTRG on the Pennsylvania market.

Another major bonus of the merger is that WTRG would receive an upgrade in its S&P credit rating from A- (currently a positive outlook, accounting for the merger) to an A grade with a stable outlook.

Combined with its sheer scale, this lower cost of capital would open up even more opportunities for the combined company to further consolidate the industry. This comes at a time when many government-owned utilities are being sold to for-profit utilities because the former can't afford the costly and necessary upgrades to their water infrastructure.

On its own, WTRG's growth outlook remains encouraging as well. Through a combination of heavy investment in capex, acquisitions, and rate increases, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted diluted EPS to rise by 6.4% in 2026 to $2.24 (following an anticipated 6.9% uptick to $2.11 in 2025). For 2027, another 6.7% jump in adjusted diluted EPS to $2.39 is being predicted (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to WTRG's Q3 2025 Earnings Press Release, WTRG's Q3 2025 10-Q Filing, WTRG's Q3 2025 Earnings Presentation, and WTRG's December 2025 Investor Presentation).

Fair Value Is Above $45 A Share

Over the last six months, WTRG's shares have delivered a 7% total return. This narrowly trailed the 9% total return logged by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time.

Believe it or not, though, I believe shares are about as attractively valued now as they were last August. The forward 12-month P/E ratio of 17.7 is up just a bit from the previous figure of 17.5. That's still roughly three standard deviations under the FAST Graphs 10-year average P/E ratio of 25.6.

I still believe that a rational fair value multiple for WTRG is 20.5 (two standard deviations less than the 10-year average P/E ratio). As a standalone company, its 6.2% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus is greater than the 10-year CAGR of 5.1%. This points to a growth profile that's holding up.

As was the case in August, the biggest challenge to the water utility's valuation multiple remains the elevated interest rate environment. The 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) is yielding 4.2%, which is well above the 10-year average yield of around 2.5%, according to Macrotrends.

Looking ahead, I believe the 10-year yield will stay in the high-3% to low-4% range. WTRG's intact growth prospects lead me to believe it can maintain its 100 to 150 basis point earnings yield spread to the 10-year U.S. Treasury. That supports my fair value P/E ratio of 20.5.

As of today, the calendar year 2026 is 7% in the books. This implies there's another 93% of 2026 and 7% of 2027 still ahead over the coming 12 months. That produces a forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input of $2.25.

Applying my fair value multiple of 20.5, I arrive at a fair value per share of $46. Against the current $40 share price (as of January 26th, 2026), this is equivalent to a 14% discount to fair value.

If WTRG returns to fair value and matches growth forecasts, it could post a 21% total return through 2026. By the end of 2030, WTRG will have a path to 11% annual total returns. If the deal with AWK is finalized, the annual total return potential would remain above 10% over the next several years.

A Reset Will Meaningfully Accelerate Dividend Growth

WTRG's 3.4% dividend yield matches the utility sector's sector median forward yield. This means its prior 20 basis point spread to the sector median has narrowed down to nothing. Still, Seeking Alpha's Quant System issues B- and C+ grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (both of which were unchanged from August).

WTRG has also proven itself to be a reliable dividend grower. As a standalone company, the water utility has upped its dividend for 34 consecutive years. That's more than triple the sector median of 10.7 years, which is sufficient to garner an A grade from the Quant System for consecutive years of dividend growth metric.

On a standalone company basis, WTRG's dividend remains quite safe. The company's adjusted diluted EPS payout ratio is poised to be in the low-60% range in 2026. That would be squarely within its targeted payout ratio range of 60% to 65%. According to the Zen Research Terminal, it's also comfortably lower than the 75% payout ratio that rating agencies prefer from the industry.

This gives it breathing room, which accounts for the low dividend cut probabilities in the Zen Research Terminal. Over the next 12 months, the risk of a payout reduction from WTRG is 0.7%. In the next average recession, that rises to still only 2%.

Of course, there's the caveat that upon the closing of the merger with AWK, WTRG's payout will effectively be cut by about 26%, according to fellow SA analyst Ian Bezek.

It's not all bad news on that front, though. This is because the Quant System anticipates 6.1% annual forward dividend per share growth from WTRG on a standalone basis. That would be about 80 basis points above the sector median of 5.3%.

Compensating for this backdoor dividend cut is the fact that dividend growth will significantly pick up off a lower starting base. The Quant System anticipates AWK will deliver 8.2% annual dividend growth to shareholders. So, on the one hand, WTRG shareholders are losing about 90 basis points of starting yield to pick up 210 basis points in long-term annual dividend growth potential.

Risks To Consider

With or without the merger with AWK, WTRG is a solid regulated utility. Even so, there are still risks to the investment thesis.

Unsurprisingly, the most relevant risk to WTRG now pertains to merger execution and integration into AWK. Getting this deal across the finish line will require the companies to navigate some obstacles.

The ones nearest on the horizon are the upcoming shareholder votes scheduled for February 10th. The results of these votes will be released by AWK and WTRG after their respective meetings conclude on that day. Overall, I expect both of these votes to approve the deal.

The biggest obstacle to the deal being completed has to do with the numerous approvals from state utility commissions. This is because combining the two largest water utilities in the country is sure to stir up controversy.

One of the clearest examples of the implications of such a merger is the current situation in Chester, Pennsylvania. The city's local sewage authority is currently up for sale, with AWK and WTRG being the only two realistic players looking to acquire the assets.

Unifying these two companies would create a powerhouse entity that would hold substantial leverage in negotiations with government-owned utilities in the future. That's due to the diminished competition in government-owned utilities acquisitions that would result from these two being combined.

This could lead to delays or potential rate freezes being the price of ultimate approval. If this deal isn't executed, the protection for WTRG is the $835 million cash termination fee to which AWK agreed as part of the merger agreement.

If the merger is finalized, the next phase for the combined company would be integration. If the combined company loses key personnel or customer service is disrupted, that could weigh on its fundamentals.

As a standalone enterprise, the risks to WTRG are basically unchanged. These include the supermajority of its rate base being concentrated in Pennsylvania (75.2% as of September 30th, 2025) and being a target of attempted cyber breaches.

The former risk could impact fundamentals if future rate cases from Pennsylvania's regulators aren't as constructive. The latter risk would present if a major cyber breach compromised sensitive customer data.

Summary: A Solid Buy For Either Merger Outcome

No matter how the pending merger with AWK plays out, WTRG looks well-positioned to generate healthy earnings growth for shareholders. As a standalone company, its balance sheet is A-rated. The dividend is also rather secure, with room to build on its 34-year payout growth streak. Shares are trading well below our fair value estimate as well.

If the deal does close, earnings growth would be even stronger for WTRG shareholders in the new AWK. The credit rating would be a notch higher from S&P than it currently is for WTRG. AWK has been a solid dividend grower in its own right. Lastly, shares are also priced at a double-digit percentage discount to my fair value. For these reasons, I'm sticking with my Buy rating.

data.title: S&P Global Isn't Cheap - But It's Still A Buy If You Think Long Term
data.description:
S&P Global (SPGI) may seem to some to be a high-quality business and an obvious buy in certain circumstances, but to me, it is not so obvious. There are some uncertainties, such as AI, competition, and regulation, and for this valuation to work, we have to consider that the company will continue to be a compounder.

But even in this context, I believe that SPGI's valuation has recently improved significantly and opened up a margin of safety. And that, with wide moats to protect against uncertainties and reinforce long-term conviction, is enough for my buy rating.

The Wonder of S&P Global: Compounding

You may have found my comment at the beginning of this article about a better valuation for SPGI, with an attractive margin of safety, strange. After all, we still see a P/E of almost 30x. But the first point is precisely that the P/E of 30x is based on the calendar year of 2025. If we look at 2026, which is where the “real” forward P/E is, we already see a multiple of 26.7x.

This may not seem like such a good multiple yet, but we have to take a few factors into account. The first is that the average forward P/E of SPGI is close to 30x, as the table below shows.

A second point is that S&P Global has already earned the term “quality business” and, consequently, needs a premium valuation consistent with that.

This is basically what happens with companies like Walmart (WMT), Costco (COST), and Apple (AAPL), where the business is so solid that the market ends up accepting a lower potential return in favor of greater stability and predictability.

But if you usually follow my other articles, most of the time I don't like these “consensus” companies, precisely because at levels like Costco, the market ends up compressing the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) a lot.

Fortunately, I don't think SPGI has reached that level, and this is mainly due to the long term.

Let's simplify this valuation a little and assume that S&P Global will continue to increase its top line consistently every year, at something close to high single digits, eventually reaching mid single digits. This effect on EPS should be catalyzed by better margins and also by buybacks. Therefore, I don't think it's difficult to say that the company can maintain EPS growth of 12% and 10% per year.

Based solely on this assumption, let's say that by 2028, EPS will grow by ~12% per year, and by 2036, it will grow by 10%. This should take S&P Global's EPS from $17.85 to $53.75. This implies a P/E below 10x and an earnings yield above 10%.

Again, you may be thinking, “That's still not enough. Am I going to wait 10 years for a shareholder yield of only 10%?” But in my view, that's more than enough to envision an interesting upside. Not only is 10% attractive for a company of this quality, but in 2036, it is very likely that the company will not have experienced such a severe multiple derating, since, in my view, it has everything it needs to remain a solid and mature company.

If the market still assigns a P/E of ~25x in 2036, a slight drop due to lower expected growth, since at that point we should have a much more consolidated S&P Global, we are still talking about an SPGI stock trading at $1,343, or an upside of 152% in 10 years. Breaking down these 10 years, we are talking about a total return of 9.9% per year.

These certainly seem to me to be more than enough prospects to sustain an optimistic view for S&P Global, with an IRR of close to double digits in the long term, through a quality company, and still with the potential to improve this compounding if execution is good enough to maintain these high growth prospects beyond 2036.

Why These Prospects For SPGI?

The first reason to be confident that the company can deliver on these prospects is because it has already proven that it can. Its track record is very good. Over the last 10 years, average revenue growth was 11%, and EPS was 50% (CAGR). If we narrow it down to 5 years, average EPS grew by 6.6% (but with very healthy net income growth, showing that this also had an effect on stock issuance and the like), but revenue remained at 15%.

Looking ahead, the market continues to estimate figures close to these, with revenue growing at ~7.5% per year over the next few years and EPS growth between 10% and 12%.

Even so, market estimates seem conservative compared to what S&P Global internally seeks to achieve. The enterprise targets are organic, constant currency revenue growth of 7% to 9% per year, with a 0.5-0.75 pps expansion in margin and double-digit EPS growth over the next 3-5 years.

Note that although expectations suggest very interesting growth for all divisions, S&P Dow Jones Indices stands out with slightly higher revenue growth, and this is already one of the reasons for having confidence in the company for the coming decades. We are talking about a secular trend, with more and more individuals and institutions using indexes, ETFs, and also with new products based on these indexes emerging, not to mention that this is revenue that grows organically, since the more the market grows, the more AUM grows, which increases revenue almost organically. It is an excellent margin with high predictability, not to mention the optionality of new indexes always emerging, increased revenue from derivatives and the like.

Although this is one of the revenues I like the most, it is only 13% of the group's total revenue. However, the other revenues are not far behind in quality. The rating business, for example, seems to me to be often underestimated. This type of service is crucial to keeping the debt industry running worldwide. It is not as simple as a corporation that issues debt paying because it wants to be evaluated, wants to provide more transparency, and make its debt more in demand. It is something that, without a rating, basically does not work, since most institutional investors would not even consider investing in unrated debt.

The same applies to the data analytics market, which is crucial for the industry. In some cases, subscribers are not even paying to gain an edge over the market, but rather to obtain the information necessary to make investments.

And all of these areas have very interesting prospects, not only for organic growth as the industry continues to grow gradually, but also with internal company initiatives, such as increasing the product shelf, increasing penetration in international markets, and using GenAI to assist in both volume and efficiency.

A great example is Energy revenue, which S&P Global managed to integrate well into the business after the M&A with IHS Markit. It is a product where the company also provides essential data (pricing, derivatives, and so on) and generates a lot of value for the customer, with 87% of revenue already recurring. Now think that in 10 years, we will likely be looking at an S&P Global with potential new sources of revenue, further monetizing the same customer, but with even better and complementary services.

There Are Uncertainties For SPGI, But...

Despite my optimism, it is necessary to mention that there are risks. The main one is competition, which is also closely related to AI today.

To begin with, S&P Global, although a benchmark in the industry, is not the only one providing this type of service. In ratings, for example, we have Fitch Ratings and Moody's (MCO). Despite this, ratings are basically an oligopoly, and this is unlikely to change due to the market structure, network effects, and reputation that all of these companies have built.

Indices are also basically an oligopoly, and I hardly think there will be a major disruption, but it is still worth considering that companies such as MSCI may become more aggressive and occasionally steal market share from S&P Global.

But it is precisely in market intelligence where I consider there to be more risks, mainly because the moats are less apparent. Of course, the strength of other revenues can help in Market Intelligence, providing support and already winning over customers, but there is clear competition from various products, from large ones such as Bloomberg to smaller platforms that are able to generate an “alternative” service to S&P's market intelligence. This also goes hand in hand with the risk that the market has recently been perceiving, which is AI.

For me, this risk is being inflated lately, mainly because, as the image below shows, most of S&P Global's revenue is proprietary data, so when we are in a more mature market, I imagine that LLMs will not be able to steal this IP or generate content based on these reports for free, with strong regulation protecting it.

But still, it is an uncertainty to be considered. I don't think ordinary LLMs will be able to take the throne from S&P's Market Intelligence, but the AI trend certainly lowers the barrier to entry. Whereas before you needed decades of expertise and a huge team, now a fintech company may be able to scale up a significant volume of data much more quickly that can minimally compete with market intelligence. I'm not saying it will be able to achieve the same volume, but perhaps enough to steal a little market share as an “alternative” for some customers.

Regulation is also a double-edged sword. As I mentioned, it ends up protecting S&P Global a lot, but there is also always some level of uncertainty about how this may change, whether it may occasionally encourage more competition or limit the market power that the big players have.

The Bottom Line

Even with these uncertainties, I believe that S&P Global has more than enough moats to defend this market and make it possible to have a high conviction that it will be able to ride these trends. And that, with an attractive valuation below the historical average, means SPGI is a buy for me.

data.title: HCA Healthcare: A Booming Buyback But Risks Loom (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of HCA Healthcare, Inc. (HCA) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining over 40%. The company has used buybacks to aggressively reduce share count while also steadily expanding operating margins, creating a favorable environment for shares. A solid Q4 and 2026 outlook sent shares surging another 7% in early trading on Tuesday. I last covered shares in October, when I rated the stock a “Hold” given strong operating performance but looming policy headwinds as Medicaid and lower ACA subsidies may begin to impact visits and bad debt expense. Since then, shares have continued to rally, adding 16% and surpassing my $450 price target for profit taking. With updated financials and such strong performance, now is a good time to revisit HCA to determine the appropriate next step for investors.

Margin Benefits May Be Nearing Their Peak

In the company's fourth quarter, HCA earned $8.01, blowing past estimates by $0.53 as revenue grew by 7% to $19.5 billion. For the full year, HCA earned $28.33 on revenue of $75.6 billion. In Q4, adjusted EBITDA was up 11% to $4.1 billion as margins expanded to 21% from 20%; this growth was slightly flattered by the fact Q4 of last year was negatively impacted by hurricanes. Still, same-facility admissions were up a strong 2.4%. Revenue per admission was up 2.9%, reflecting a strong pricing environment.

Salaries and benefits were just 42.8% of revenue, a 50bp improvement from last year. Increased utilization of its system, given higher admissions, drove some of this. Additionally, a looser labor market has helped to alleviate wage pressures, allowing HCA to recapture margin. This has been the case for about two years now, and I do believe the vast majority of this benefit has occurred. Still, the labor market is sluggish; there may be room for a slight reduction in labor costs in 2026.

Payer mix is a critical factor to consider in the hospital sector, as hospitals generally receive the highest payments from private insurers, then Medicare, and low payments from Medicaid. HCA gets about 12% of its revenue from Medicare, a third from Medicare, and about half from private insurers. The 2025 tax bill has many measures impacting Medicaid, and this ~12% of the business is likely to face pressure. Many of these measures are implemented in 2027-2029, reducing the near-term impact, but I view ~2%-4% of HCA profits ultimately being at risk. 2026 Medicaid changes and ACA subsidy cuts are likely a 1%-2% headwind this year.

Given America's aging demographics, it is not surprising that hospital revenue as a share of total personal consumption has been steadily rising. Now, this has been especially strong in 2025, passing 2.2% for the first time. This, in my view, partly reflects catch-up. In the immediate aftermath of the COVID pandemic, there were restrictions on many nonessential surgeries, creating a backlog of demand that is now being worked through. This has created a favorable operating environment for HCA. I expect that much of the backlog has been worked through, and demand growth may moderate in 2026, even excluding impact from policy changes.

Cash Flow Enables Aggressive Buybacks

Now, HCA is an extremely cash-generative business with $7.7 billion of free cash flow in 2025. With this cash flow, HCA can aggressively return capital to investors. It pays just a 0.7% dividend even with the increase to $0.78 from $0.72 and instead focuses capital returns on buybacks. It did $2.6 billion of buybacks in Q4, and thanks to these repurchases, its share count was down 9.7% from last year. The company also authorized a $10 billion buyback program. While the entire authorization may not be used within one year, I expect the vast majority to be used.

HCA can focus so much capital on shareholder returns because the company also maintains a solid balance sheet. It has $1 billion of cash and $46.5 billion of debt, resulting in ~2.9x leverage. I view ~3x as a suitable level to run the business, meaning HCA can use all 2026 free cash flow for additional buybacks. That appears to be the plan given its buyback authorization and dividend increase. I expect $9-$10 billion of buybacks this year.

Guidance Is Encouraging, But Policy Risks Loom

Alongside results, HCA provided guidance for 2026. This is closely watched as 2026 will be the first year when the company will start to feel the impact of 2025 policy changes regarding Medicaid coverage and ACA subsidies. In particular, the Medicaid pressure will build over several years given the staggered implementation of the law. Frankly, the guidance was very encouraging. Management is targeting about $78.5 billion of revenue and over $30 in EPS.

This guidance points to about 4.5% revenue growth, which is a bit slower than the 7% we saw in 2025. Some deceleration is to be expected given the policy changes to subsidies and Medicaid as well as the fact we have “caught up” from deferred procedures, as discussed earlier. Still, this is a healthy pace of revenue growth, reflecting the fact HCA has less rural and Medicaid exposure than smaller peers. EBITDA growth is expected to be a bit slower at 2.9%, pointing to some margin compression. There will undoubtedly be some reimbursement pressure this year, but I view this guidance as somewhat conservative.

HCA ended the year strongly, and the company has successfully rebuilt margins after facing pressure post-COVID. Its solid balance sheet enables capital returns, and the company has been one of the more aggressive share repurchasers in the market, effectively reducing its share count in an accretive fashion.

Valuation Is Now Full, And I Recommend Taking Profits

Investors will sigh a breath of relief that the pace of deceleration is fairly modest, reflecting ongoing market share gains from smaller providers as well as the fact that legislative changes were implemented in a phased way reducing the near-term impact. That said, investors should recognize that with these policies being staggered, the lack of 2026 impact does not mean there will never be an impact. Still, the impact is clearly manageable thus far.

Given my view that we may see faster share count reduction and that margin guidance is a bit conservative, I see HCA earning $30-$32.50 in 2026 with at least $7 billion of free cash flow. With shares up to ~$500 in early trading, the stock is about 16x earnings with a ~6% free cash flow yield. Clearly, my "Hold" rating was too cautious, as ongoing buybacks and earnings growth have been able to propel its multiple beyond my expectations (I have used a 7% free cash flow yield).

This valuation makes me cautious. To be clear, I believe HCA is a strong operator and an excellent allocator of capital. However, there are ongoing policy headwinds, and given the U.S. fiscal situation, payer reimbursement is likely to be a secular focus. Indeed, the government is seeking to hold Medicare Advantage payments flat in 2027. While initially a pressure on insurers, this will likely ultimately result in pressure on providers like HCA. Over a 3-5 year window, there are more likely to be downward pressures on margins than upward opportunities, in my view.

Increasingly, I fear that shares are not sufficiently discounting this risk. While I expect only modest margin compression in 2026, these pressures will be more apparent in future years as policy changes are fully enacted. This risk should cap valuation, and at a 6% free cash flow yield, I am cautious that investors are looking past that risk. At the same time, ongoing buybacks will help to support shares and may simply allow valuation to stay a bit higher than I previously believed, especially given the favorable outlook.

I find myself torn between keeping HCA at a "Hold" or moving it to a "Sell," and this is a very close call, especially as it will take some time for 2027–2029 pressures to be apparent. While I fully respect investors who continue to hold HCA given buybacks and its strong capital returns, discipline on valuation and my view that medium-term pressure looms lead me to move shares to a sell. We are now 10% above my exit target, and continuing to hold comes with more downside risk than upside opportunity, in my view. As such, I would recommend taking profits. However, I would use pullbacks to $450 to get back into HCA as this is a good company, just one trading at an elevated valuation.

data.title: Affirm: Groceries On Credit? The Shift In BNPL And The Credit Market
data.description:
Introduction

There is a lot of noise in the market right now regarding the Buy Now, Pay Later, or BNPL, industry. If you scroll through investor forums or analyst notes, you’ll see it’s a deeply polarizing topic. Many critics look at these companies and see a predatory model, arguing that they essentially thrive by trapping people in cycles of debt through hidden costs or high late fees (if you don’t believe me, go to the comment sections for any BNPL company on SA, and check for yourself).

But in early 2026, I think it is actually the perfect moment to look at the mechanics of this industry, especially since the recent discussions around a 10% interest rate cap on credit cards have the entire fintech sector discuss this topic, with many people who support it, and many of those who disagree.

Today’s discussion comes with me looking at Affirm Holdings, Inc. (AFRM), which I rated as a buy a while back, and a couple of other BNPL stocks I’ll briefly mention. Affirm is in the middle of it for me, because since my article, the stock has been frustratingly flat, even dipping slightly, while the broader S&P 500 (SP500) has climbed about 6%.

It is definitely a bet that hasn't played out in the short term as I expected, but it puts us in a good position to ask why. Then you have Klarna (KLAR), which I covered during its 2025 IPO. I actually warned people to avoid it at the time, and the stock has since collapsed by more than 40%.

We also need to talk about PayPal (PYPL), which has been plunging and drawing a lot of "bottom-fishing" interest, as well as smaller players like Sezzle (SEZL) and Shift4 Payments (FOUR), both of which have seen their valuations slashed significantly.

Buy Now, Pay Later As An Industry

The fundamental debate around BNPL is whether it actually helps consumers or just lures them into spending money they don't have. Critics argue that splitting a purchase into four parts doesn't magically create wealth, it just masks the reality of what you can afford. And they aren't wrong. Many of these companies have to set aside "provisions for credit losses" because they know a portion of their users simply won't be able to pay them back. However, if you are financially disciplined, these services can be a genuine lifeline.

At the same time, I recently saw a research piece from the Motley Fool that highlighted a worrying trend: 32% of people aged 18 to 29 have already missed a BNPL payment this year, compared to just 12% for those over 60. It seems the younger generation is losing track of these debts.

In fact, nearly 25% of millennials admit to forgetting they even made a purchase through a BNPL app. This is the danger of the "multiple app" ecosystem. Unlike a traditional credit card, where all your debt is visible in one monthly statement, a BNPL user might have five different apps with dozens of tiny payments scheduled at different times, which is an accounting nightmare, especially for someone who is not used to being responsible for their own financials.

What is even more concerning is the shift in what people are actually buying. We used to think of BNPL for extraordinarily big purchases like a laptop, some clothing, or a bag from a luxury brand (funny enough, even the companies positioned themselves for those needs from the beginning), but as of 2026, roughly 25% of users are now using these services for basic necessities like groceries, rent, and medical bills. Affirm and some of the others even want to launch features specifically to split monthly rent or utility payments. When people start financing their bread at 30% late-payment interest rates (in some cases, it really gets to over 30%), we naturally ask ourselves about the financial health of the consumers. However, being an analyst and covering different areas of the market, I can’t say the consumer is in distress. Or at least, based on the retail sales numbers reported by giants like Walmart (WMT) or Amazon (AMZN). It doesn’t look like it.

My view on this is nuanced. I am used to the "tashlumim" system in Israel, which translates to English as “payments,” where literally every merchant allows you to divide your purchase into 3-24 parts (depending on the size of the purchase) at 0% interest. Payments go through your regular credit card. You don’t need any external apps, and you see those purchases every time you open your banking app. I cannot imagine the Israeli economy functioning without it, and I frequently use it myself for large purchases.

When you have to buy something big and expensive, like a new fridge, most people can’t afford it right away. A good fridge (at least the one I am choosing right now for myself) costs a few thousand dollars, and it is more than I want to spend out of a single month's income. In that case, splitting it into interest-free payments is a great way of solving this issue. But the responsibility lies with the adult to ensure they aren't making so many $200 monthly payments that they start the next month already in the red. And for this, our system is linked to our banks. The bank will “lock in” the whole sum on your credit score that is left to be paid (first month, let’s say it’s $3000, the next $2800, then $2600), and it will slowly go down as long as you are paying for it. This way, if you exaggerate with overdraft, the bank will block you from continuing spending, because your credit limit will be reached, and everyone has a credit limit here.

Returning to the U.S., the government’s talk of a 10% interest rate cap on credit cards sounds well-intentioned but could be disastrous for the very people it aims to help. If banks can’t price for risk, they will simply stop lending to lower-income individuals. Those people will then be pushed even further to BNPL providers or, even worse, payday lenders.

I don't have a perfect solution for the U.S. market because I live in a country that is much more socialistic than capitalistic, and the mechanisms we use here likely wouldn't be helpful in the U.S., but what I can say for certain is that the consumer clearly needs these options for credit debt or splitting into payments. As long as the world continues to move toward online shopping, which I believe will only grow as businesses move more aggressively to digital advertising and consumers grow more reliant on the convenience of the Internet, this industry will only be going up.

Buy Now, Pay Later will grow together with this industry. So, with that context in mind, I suggest we take a look at the first major player in this space, Affirm.

Affirm

When we look at Affirm specifically, the results were much more aggressive than what you would imagine by the sideways stock price movement. I am most impressed with GAAP profitability that continues for the second quarter in a row. They reported an operating income of $64 million for fiscal Q1 '26, the quarter ending September 30, 2025.

To put this in perspective, two years ago in the same quarter, they were posting a GAAP operating loss of $209 million, and things did not look as bright. Their Gross Merchandise Volume is at $10.8 billion, representing a massive 42% year-over-year growth. Revenue jumped to $933 million, a 34% increase, while active consumers grew to 24.1 million users. One of the most important metrics for long-term health is transactions per active consumer, which rose to 6.1, up 20%. This shows Affirm is moving from a loan tool for one purchase to a recurring daily payment method, which is great for the company, but I am not sure what it says about the consumer.

A major driver of this is the Affirm Card, which has an adoption rate that surprises me. Active consumers using the card grew 101% year-over-year to 2.8 million, while the card's specific GMV surged 135% to $1.4 billion. The card attach rate is now at 12%, meaning over one in ten users is now carrying a physical or virtual card.

Interestingly, 72% of Affirm's product composition is actually interest-bearing, which is a crucial distinction from competitors like Klarna. Affirm acts more like a modern bank, earning $454 million in interest income this quarter alone. Their credit quality is steady, with an allowance for credit losses at $426 million, or 5.9% of total loans held for investment. Their 30+ day delinquency rate was 2.8% as of September, which actually tracks lower than several major traditional credit card issuers.

Looking ahead, Affirm’s outlook for 2026 is very ambitious, targeting GMV of more than $47.5 billion and an Adjusted Operating Margin of over 27.1%. It looks like the stock trades sideways due to the steep valuation and the worry of the market about the regulatory caps, but Affirm is proving it can grow volume by 40% while expanding profit margins. The valuation when accounting for the growth is already not that high, but the regulations could create headwinds for BNPL stocks. Anyway, I am still bullish on Affirm’s long-term.

Klarna

Mentioning other names, Klarna predictably dipped since its IPO by more than 40%. Here is what I said in the conclusion of my previous Klarna breakdown:

I am not sure that I would buy this stock right now, even if it were fairly valued compared to the sector, which is at about 3 times forward sales. I just see many uncertainties, and I still have many questions that will be answered only after this company shows a strong couple of quarters as a public company.

After an almost 50% drop from its peak, Klarna is at the valuation I said I am not sure I would buy into.

Right now, their revenue of $903 million beat estimates, but they are still struggling with an adjusted operating loss of $14 million. What I find interesting about Klarna, though, is that it isn't a U.S.-based company. It's based in Sweden, and it gets a different perspective on regulation and consumer protection. They are working toward everyday payments, with 32% of their transactions now falling into that category. Now, I can justify the case for Klarna much more than I did back then. However, until they can prove that their AI-driven cost-cutting can take them to profitability, I would be careful. They have the scale with 114 million users, but the stock is in a free fall in this skeptical market.

PayPal

PayPal is the stock that everyone loves to hate lately, but their Q3 2025 results were actually quite solid. Revenue got to $8.42 billion, and they initiated a dividend, which means that they are maturing into a "cash cow" rather than a high-growth tech darling. Their own BNPL volumes are growing, but they are facing massive pressure in their "branded checkout." The stock has been beaten down because its transaction margins have shrunk as they process more unbranded payments.

Cash flow to sales is on a decline, and investors are worried that larger players are squeezing this company out of the market. PayPal looks like a value play right now, although I have to be honest, I don’t really like or understand this company. It’s a personal preference that I acquired a very long time ago, during the pandemic in 2020. I don’t remember what exactly happened, but I decided not to ever use PayPal after that (I think I got annoyed by the high commission). So just like that, it is easy sometimes to lose a customer forever.

Back to the BNPL discussion, PYPL is not "exciting" like Affirm, but with $6 billion in expected share repurchases this year, they are doing everything they can to support the stock price while they figure out how to innovate again.

Sezzle

Sezzle recently hit its first $1 billion GMV quarter, with revenue jumping 67%. Unlike the bigger players, Sezzle is focusing heavily on subscription models like "Sezzle Premium," which builds more predictable revenue. However, because they are smaller, they are much more sensitive to shifts in consumer credit quality. Even after the stock is down more than 60% in the last 6 months, based on the Price to Sales ratio, it is much more expensive than Klarna and not that far away from Affirm, which means the stock could possibly see even bigger downside.

Shift4

Moving to Shift4 Payments, we see a stock that has been down nearly 45% as investors and analysts struggle to find a consensus on the true health of the business. Shift4 is a bit of a different beast compared to the pure-play BNPL firms, because they manage payment processing for difficult environments like hotels or stadiums. The massive drop in the stock price seems to be a reaction to the complexity of their growth-by-acquisition strategy. They’ve been aggressively buying up companies (most notably the Global Blue acquisition), which led to a reported 61% revenue growth in recent periods. However, the market is looking past that number and worrying about the organic growth, which sits at about 19%, which is not that impressive.

We are kind of looking at the two different businesses: one that is a high-growth fintech disruptor and another that is a heavily leveraged consolidator. The low valuation multiple reflects the worries of the market.

Conclusion

I am not the kind of person who can strike a deal with my conscience and ignore a “dirty” business or go against my principles just to make money. I’ve never been that way. While I understand the arguments people make against Buy Now, Pay Later companies, I don't actually agree that their profits are built solely on tricking people. At the end of the day, I believe every adult is responsible for their own financial health and education, and these companies work exclusively with the 18-plus population. Of course, the government could help with better policies, and companies could vet their users more strictly, but if an adult chooses to make a purchase they cannot afford, who is to blame but themselves?

Because of this, I rated Affirm as a Buy and will continue to do so because I believe in the long-term story. They are poised to hold a significant share of a growing industry, and I expect the company to scale right alongside that market growth over time.

Regarding other players, I really like Klarna simply because it offers a way to diversify away from a U.S.-only portfolio. It is definitely a name to keep on your watch list as it continues going down. Waiting for a clear rebound or a period of consolidation rather than catching a falling knife is likely the smarter strategy there.

As for PayPal, the results weren't bad, and it looks like a strong value opportunity, but I’m personally not buying it. I only invest in businesses I find truly compelling and interesting, and PayPal just doesn't spark that for me right now.

Finally, when it comes to the other names that have plunged, like Sezzle or Shift4, they simply aren't to my taste. Between the current market sentiment, the momentum, and the specific ways those companies are choosing to grow, I don't find them to be the right fit for my portfolio.

data.title: BITW: Delivering Cryptocurrencies In A Single Fund
data.description:
If we analyze the Bitwise 10 Crypto Index Fund ETF (BITW), at first glance it appears as a rather “rare” solution to gain an all-in-one exposure to the crypto market. An exposure that potentially presents itself as a summary of the market, which also introduces other holdings beyond Bitcoin and Ethereum. And if, by limiting ourselves to this, only positive aspects seem to emerge, this overall view of BITW can instead be very helpful in understanding the real pros and cons.

Introduction and Definition

BITW is a product sponsored by Bitwise Investment Advisers, LLC, launched in November 2017, initially as a private placement and then as a public solution from 2025. Today, with AUM above $1 billion, with custody service Coinbase Custody Trust Company, which is run by Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN).

It was created with the objective of tracking the Bitwise 10 Large Cap Crypto Index, “with exceptions determined by the Sponsor at its sole discretion”; however, it has a fairly pronounced tracking error, rated F by SA (irrelevant turnover).

For the buyer, there is an expense ratio of 0.75%, to which is added a median bid–ask spread (30d) of ~0.26%. The accrual is daily, and fees are payable in cash or in crypto; when in crypto, the fee progressively reduces the reserves per share (reason why presumably there is such a wide tracking error).

From a bureaucratic standpoint, the fund still appears today to have a non-investment company legal structure under the 1940 Act (mandatory K-1) with tax allocation on a monthly basis.

What Does BITW Do?

The fund seeks to provide exposure in a single instrument to the main cryptocurrencies. In doing so, it does not seek alpha, or at least it has never declared it, and in fact, it does not carry out active management; rather, it positions itself as a purely directional solution on the crypto market.

In detail, the benchmark selects the top ten assets by adjusted market value, applying eligibility criteria that impose a base level of liquidity, regulated custody, and the exclusion of stablecoin/memecoin. To limit the classic revolving door effect, the following are introduced:

  • 10% tolerance thresholds
  • Corrective procedures under extraordinary circumstances in the presence of stressed market conditions

These filters do not, however, reduce the standard deviation or the instability of the portfolio; on the contrary, annual volatility surpasses that of alternative ETFs, justifying the “F” rating of Seeking Alpha.

Who is BITW for?

These characteristics make it more consistent with a medium time horizon to take advantage of the more favorable phases of the crypto cycle.

It is designed as a core solution for the crypto segment of a portfolio and potentially as a satellite of a portfolio, even if its characteristics make it suitable for tactical and cyclical exposures. This instrument has no hedging or protection purpose, nor does it represent a real alternative to strategies concentrated exclusively on Bitcoin. Moreover, the absence of staking mechanisms prevents the generation of returns on the held assets, effectively making it poorly suited for those seeking periodic income streams.

Therefore, BITW seems to be designed for investors who want simplified crypto exposure or accept structural complexity and high volatility.

How is BITW Built?

Inside BITW, the weight is in fact heavily concentrated in Bitcoin (~75%) and Ethereum (~15%). The rest of the portfolio weighs less than 10%. The exposure is therefore effectively BTC + ETH > 90%, and the marginal contribution of altcoins is almost irrelevant in terms of risk/return.

And in this sense it is interesting to know that the inclusion mechanism follows the market-weight logic of the crypto segment, and the same applies to the rebalancing mechanism. This makes the process highly momentum-driven. Conceptually, it becomes primarily a Bitcoin and Ethereum vehicle with some rebalancing noise. By hypothesis, in a crypto regime:

  • If BTC increases dominance, then BITW becomes increasingly weighted to BTC.
  • If BTC decreases in dominance (usually when altcoins rally), then BITW rebalances toward more volatile assets that have appreciated, increasing BITW's risk profile.

This makes the index pro-cyclical, not neutral, and many names that yesterday represented the backbone of the market, therefore positions included inside BITW in the past, today are no longer there, such as EOS, Dash, Bitcoin Cash, Ethereum Classic, Monero, etc.

Risks

The fund therefore has a high directional exposure to the crypto market, specifically an almost total correlation with Bitcoin (97%). Therefore, in the first place is market risk, in the sense that the crypto segment is highly volatile and dependent on the halving cycle (at least up to today).

Its high beta nature relative to Bitcoin makes it potentially more profitable in expansive phases, but also less conservative in contractionary ones. In terms of Bitcoin, this already assumes pronounced volatility metrics (annualized volatility above 47%). For this reason, over the long period, BITW tends to underperform Bitcoin.

At this point, it therefore also makes sense to consider the cyclicality of Bitcoin: a highly cyclical asset that alternates bullish phases with pronounced bearish phases, which up to today has been called the “halving cycle,” but which has nonetheless coincided with cyclical phases of global monetary change.

This has amplified the correlation with traditional assets, primarily equities.

Therefore, if Bitcoin is a sort of high beta of the equity market, and BITW a sort of high beta of Bitcoin, the expected volatility (based on past performance) of BITW is presumably higher.

Peer Comparison

Having made these considerations, it is therefore natural to compare BITW with single ETF alternatives. Among the solutions, I selected Grayscale Bitcoin Mini Trust ETF (BTC) and the Grayscale Ethereum Mini Staking (ETH):

An interesting consideration is if BITW positions itself as an intermediate solution between BTC and ETH, it should be considered that in terms of TER, investing in BITW would cost more than replicating the same allocation (75–15%) between BTC and ETH. Then, in terms of replicability, it must be said that BITW confirms itself as a midpoint in terms of volatility between BTC and ETH ETFs:

And also with regard to momentum, its hybrid nature between BTC and ETH is confirmed, still considering a 1Y total return of -9.60% generated by the drawdown of Bitcoin in parallel with a “classic” increase in dominance of alternative solutions such as ETFs.

All of this is well represented by the parallelism of SA “ETF grades,” which express higher momentum for BITW but a higher expense ratio, even though all three share the same risk grade.

Of course, these grades may change over time as performance fluctuates.

Pros and Cons

In summary, BITW presents some well-defined strengths:

  • It facilitates access to the crypto-asset market by replicating the Bitwise 10 Large Cap Crypto Index
  • The index follows a rigorous and non-discretionary approach; this increases the predictability of returns.
  • It seems to behave like a high beta of Bitcoin (thanks to the contribution in performance from Ethereum), which (partially) amplifies the size of positive oscillations compared to solutions oriented only toward Bitcoin.

At the same time, some structural criticalities are not lacking:

  • Diversification is theoretical: in practice the limited weight of altcoins has little impact on the overall outcome.
  • The level of costs cannot be neglected compared to Bitcoin/Ethereum-oriented solutions.
  • Its high beta nature brings with it high volatility and pronounced drawdowns. This cycle, however, has negative effects over the long term.

This article answers three main questions about BITW:

  1. How is BITW's portfolio structured, and what is its relationship to cryptocurrency cyclicality?
  2. What risks accompany BITW?
  3. How can BITW be used in a portfolio?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Adidas: Updating For 2026 With A 'Buy'
data.description:
Adidas used to be a holding in my long-term portfolio. If you follow my articles on the company, you can see that I bought at about $85/share in 2022, when the company dropped as low as below $60/share. I did some slight buying following that article (not giving the company any sort of major position in my portfolio, but a 1.5-2% one) which in the short-to-medium term, worked out fairly well. Adidas, as we know now, climbed beyond about $130/share for the ADDYY ADR ticker which is the one I have been covering. In late 2024, together with other exits, I also exited Adidas, keeping only what I consider or call a "watchlist position" (a couple of shares, less than 0.15% of my total). I rotated because a combination of short-term upside, and because I saw challenges in Adidas' (as well as Nike (NKE) and other footwear companies), and didn't want to be part of the further journey.

Now, about 4 years later after the article you can find here, Adidas is dropping back down.

The purpose of this article is to determine the reason for that drop, to decide whether it's a good time to invest and get back in, or whether the current macroeconomic circumstances mean that it's a good idea to stay out of the company, and maybe the sector.

What better company to choose as a target for such an approach than Adidas? The company is, in my estimate, a leader not only in footwear, but athletic and sports apparel. I believe the company has a moat based on its brand (meaning its intangible assets), and while the company clearly has had issues, reflected by the company's performance, the potential for recovery is something we are looking at here.

Let's see what we have going into 2026E.

Adidas - Updating for 2026E with a potential for recovery

It's been some time since I updated on Adidas. So what's happened? The past few years has seen more issues than upside. The company has been struggling, among other things, with Inventory management especially in the NA region, as well as top-line sales declines also in the US region for two years running. The increased competitive pressure especially in the US was part of my reason for forecasting Adidas at a lower target, and to ultimately rotate the position at a price with over 40% RoR.

Part of it is also simple product dynamics. Adidas made a good chunk of its sales and results with products that are losing popularity, in the sub-segment called "casual footwear". This is what opened or what made Adidas appeal not only to specialized footwear or products, but to everyday use, bridging the distance between what was once a clear distinction between casual/everyday and sport. For many years, casual sneakers seemed to have replaced more formal footwear in professional and other settings, but this seems to be over.

The company has further seen a slowdown in its Terrace footwear, which is also maturing - meaning that the intense, initial-stage growth is done.

If we combine this with the simple fact that there's very intense competition in footwear and casualwear/sportwear, it puts Adidas products, which are usually sold at a premium price, in a difficult spot. I cover ON Holding, and this is a brand that is directly taking on Adidas even here. There were trends that were sub-brand specific, such as what happened with the Yeezy brand, the end of a multi-year saga, which certainly had to do with valuation trends as well as my decision to sell at what I believed to be an attractive price point (for selling, that is).

The above-mentioned trends caused a flurry of downgrades from major analyst houses, which in turn also affected the company's share price. And that, dear readers, is where we are today.

Adidas has good "parts" of the business as well, of course. I will go into why I view the company as attractive in the valuation segment, but performance-wise, the underlying results for Adidas were actually very good. This is reflected by the latest, 3Q25 results, which saw 12% YoY growth in top-line sales, which comes to the highest net sales quarter in Adidas company history. Both profit margins as well as operating profit, was up. YTD, the trends are even better, with a net sales percentage profit in the double digits. This might not sound like much, but for a company like Adidas, it's significant.

Furthermore, trends in every single geography, even in NA, are up.

This means that there needs to be a very good reason why the company hasn't moved back up in valuation again - because if there isn't, that could mean that this company could have an impressive upside. This is especially true because of the company's as-of-yet less tapped market potential in its sales channel. Over 60% of the company's mix is still wholesale. An improvement to higher sales numbers in DTC and e-commerce will further improve these margins.

I categorize Adidas as a footwear company, and speak a lot about the footwear. The reason for this is that Adidas, still at over 55% of sales, is a footwear company. Only 36% is apparel, and accessories are below 10%, making them relatively unimportant. The popularity of the sports and activities that make Adidas major sales, such as Football, Running/Gym and basketball, are showing no signs of abating either - the opposite.

And as I've cited before in previous articles, the importance of marketing and partnering with both individual stars as well as teams and institutions are central to the company's appeal in some of these areas - and despite everything (including tariffs), these trends are showing positivity as well.

I try to spend more time on what's going on underneath the hood.

In terms of gross margin drivers, FX and Tariffs have been heavy in their impact on the company. Without that, the improvement in GM would have pushed the company above 52%. That being said, Adidas has been able to save on production costs, reduce freight costs back down, and is seeing a more favorable business mix as well. The company is investing more in marketing, as percentage of sales, and the improvements in operating margin and profit are coming as a result of overhead expense improvement.

What does this mean? The company is improving inventory trends, it's using digital tools more, it's basically doing what most companies seem to be doing - and it's one of the reasons why the company recently bumped its outlook for fiscal 2025.

When considered in this context, the company seems very attractive. Adidas has impressive control over cost and pricing, and the increase in digital capabilities is also solid. With €4B in e-commerce sales at only 18% of total, the pathway for more is clear. The company is a leading sponsor of many teams around the world, and I believe the lack of popularity of football (what NA calls 'soccer') is impacting things here. Furthermore, Adidas bulls are citing upside in the Chinese market, where Adidas has only 10% market share.

If you follow my work, you'll know I like many of the positives, but give little regard for the China argument. Forget local brands like N-Ling, and forget for a moment even the more serious Xinjiang cotton controversy. I believe China is not a market where a company should expect significant long-term growth, but more importantly, stability. Between lockdowns, slowed consumer spending due to a housing and economic downturn, inflation in pricing and costs, and operational and high inventory-related issues, I don't think China would be counted on.

At the very least, what I will do is to forecast Adidas without a significant rise and growth from China, choosing instead to forecast China at a low single-digit growth rate.

Beyond that, though, the company is currently trading in direct opposition to the implied results trajectory, meaning it's trading down while results are forecasted to go up.

This is always a good position to "get into", if the company is good.

Valuation for Adidas - Attractive in some forecasts, less so in others.

The company's risks are primarily related to production, to global tariffs, and to the company's future strategy. Much of the core of the business is solid, however, and saying that the company isn't attractive here would be arguing that the company is unlikely to grow further. I believe that to be unlikely - arguing instead that growth is likely from here, and that at a multiple of about 16-18x P/E for the 2025 results, the company is at the very least attractively valued here.

Direct peers are Nike, Puma (PUM) and maybe On holding. I would view Nike as not attractively valued here, despite better fundamentals at A+ with a higher yield. The company's growth problems and challenges have their own implications, and I don't see a good upside to any conservative, sub-25x P/E. Puma might be a bit more of a contrarian bet - but to me it's too contrarian, with recent 2025E AEPS estimates going negative, for both that year and for the 2026E fiscal. I'm comfortable sitting out and letting that company do "it's thing" to see where it goes.

That leaves Adidas. The current AEPS estimate for the company carries us to €10.6 in 2026 (native ticker), €12.4 in 2027, and close to €15/share in 2028E. If anything close to this materializes, which based on historical accuracy is far from impossible, then this company has impressive upside and appeal here. The company is currently trading substantially below both historical and other peer averages, weighted at 17-18x, and this is when put next to 2025 results, not 2028. At 2028, it would trade below 12-13x.

My issue is China and risks. I discount Adidas forecasts and estimates by 20% per year, forecasting 12-16% instead of 22-25% growth. I do this because I don't believe in a China recovery, the Chinese market as a growth driver for Adidas, and because of the company's relatively centralized and not diversified production. The company's above-average ROIC does not change my outlook. I further believe such a significant discount puts a very conservative view on the potential for further tariffs. I work with a 4-5% sales growth per year, a 8-9% OM, with more of the company's earnings coming from e-commerce, which will improve margins. For 2026, a recovery year, I work with an EPS forecast of €10.6 at 9.5% sales growth. I work with a 2028E fair value of €250/share, which puts the company at less than 14-15x P/E for that year - based on 2026E, I'd want to pay no more than the current multiple for 2026E results, which comes to 16-17x, or €165/share.

My shorter-term P/E is therefore €165, working from a long-term fair value target of at least €250/share, based on an extremely conservative forecast. Just to illustrate for you how conservative this is, take a look at the prospective returns at 24x p/E for 2026E.

And Adidas or Nike do not trade at 24-25x P/E - their historicals put them above 30x, in Adidas' case, the company's 20-year average is 32x, which puts it at an estimated FV of close to €500/share.

This should illustrate to you just how conservative I am staying here.

For ADDYY, my target of €165/share comes to $97.5/share.

Risks & Considerations

The risks to Adidas are, in my view, relatively easy to understand. From a fundamental standpoint, the company's production base is incredibly localized, with over 90% in Asia. This means that any protectionist push, including but not limited to US tariffs, has significant potential impacts on the company's profits and margins.

There's a very general risk of increased competition from increasingly specialized, smaller, and more agile providers and manufacturers as well, with local and regional competition apparent from every geography that the company operates in - from the EU, to NA, to China, and others.

Thirdly, Adidas has, unlike its competition, not a clear lifestyle brand that the company works with. This was the idea behind the Yeezy brand - and since 2022, when that brand was basically discontinued/cancelled, Adidas has been unable to replace those sales. That's one of the reasons for the above-mentioned decline in results, and another risk to the company here.

Thesis

That being said, I believe the upside for Adidas going into 2026 is very strong. I may not be a big-time investor in apparel any longer, but I don't ignore an illogical market reaction such as this one. Even in the case of a complete lack of premium, Adidas is attractive here. Even just forecasted at 15x P/E, the company is very likely to outperform here.

Based on its A-rated credit, its nearly 1.4% yield, its market capitalization, and even discounting by 20% for the lack of China (my estimate, not the markets), I come to a €12/share AEPS for the year of 2028, step-by-step improvements from about €7.5 in 2025.

My PT for the company is below €165/share, which comes to a 22x 2025A P/E, but which would represent less than 12x P/E for 2028, and a fair value at about €250/share, representing 15-17x P/E for 2028. Based on that, I see a potential for market-beating returns.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €140/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UnitedHealth, Humana: CMS' Rate Decision Has Crushed Stock, & There's No Quick Fix
data.description:
Investment Overview

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) and Humana Inc. (HUM) shareholders have much to be unhappy about today, as in early trading today, the former's share price is down >19%, to ~$285 per share, while the latter's is down ~20%, to $210 (at the time of writing).

These companies are the two largest providers of Medicare Advantage ("MA") healthcare plans for Americans aged over 65, an industry that was once characterized by rapid growth and wide profit margins, but which may have just had the rug pulled from under it by the Trump administration, it seems.

MA plans are paid for by the U.S. government and administered by private health insurers such as UnitedHealth and Humana - as I wrote in a recent note covering UnitedHealth's Q3 earnings:

According to KFF.org, an independent research organization, ~54% of eligible Medicare beneficiaries are enrolled in MA plans - ~34.1m people out of 62.8m eligible. That's up from ~19% in 2007. The share is forecast to increase to ~64% by 2034, and ~70% of all enrollees have independent plans, which are open for general enrollment.

UnitedHealth has 29% of all MA enrollees - ~9.9m members, with Humana second, 17%, or 5.7m enrollees, and CVS third with 12%, or ~4.1m members. Centene has ~1m members.

The equation is essentially straightforward - spend less on a patient's healthcare in any given year than the fee received from the U.S. government, and the insurer pockets the difference. Spend more, and the insurer loses money.

At first, it was the health insurers which came out on top. By my calculation, UnitedHealth and Humana's operating margins in 2020 were 6.7% and 8.7% respectively. UnitedHealth has other businesses aside from MA, for example its Optum RX Pharmacy Benefit Manager ("PBM") business and Optum Health divisions, but it's likely MA would have been the most lucrative of its divisions.

In 2023, Humana's operating margin was a still-healthy 4.6%, while UnitedHealth's remained at ~8.7%. In 2024, the Centers for Medicaid and Medicare Services ("CMS") opted to increase rate payments for MA plans for 2025 by just 3.7%, which insurers interpreted as an overall reduction, given rising healthcare utilization.

Humana's operating margin sank to 2.7% in 2024, and in Q3 2025, ~2%. Announcing its full-year earnings pre-market today, UnitedHealth reported a figure of 5.36%, and guided for a figure of 5.5% in 2026.

Between November 2023 and November 2025, Humana stock fell from ~$515 per share, to $227 per share, down >55%, while between April 2025 and July 2025, UnitedHealth stock fell from $600 per share to ~$240 per share, down ~60%.

In short, MA plans had become less profitable as a result of lower than expected rate rises from the CMS, and the market began to panic, but the agency opted for a larger increase of >5% for 2026, and many analysts - myself included - believed that a fair compromise had been reached and that UnitedHealth and Humana stock would mount a comeback.

Yesterday, however, the Trump administration dropped its bombshell - a proposed rate increase for 2027 of just 0.09%. In a statement, the CMS commented:

CMS also expects that the CY 2027 Advance Notice’s proposed policies will promote even greater payment accuracy, maintain beneficiary choice, help ensure affordable coverage for Medicare beneficiaries, and enable an even more stable MA program in the long run.
